perturbation.original_text: The receiving party will maintain the confidentiality of the Confidential Information of the disclosing party with at least the same degree of care that it uses to protect its own confidential and proprietary information, but no less than a reasonable degree of care under the circumstances.
perturbation.changed_text: The receiving party will endeavor to maintain the confidentiality of the Confidential Information of the disclosing party with a degree of care they deem appropriate.
perturbation.explanation: The modification weakens the obligation to maintain confidentiality by changing 'will maintain' to 'will endeavor to maintain' and replacing the objective 'reasonable degree of care under the circumstances' with the subjective 'degree of care they deem appropriate.' This introduces ambiguity and makes enforcement difficult.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. Changing the obligation to 'a degree of care they deem appropriate' allows for a potentially lower standard of care than 'reasonable under the circumstances,' potentially failing to meet the 'reasonable efforts' standard required by CUTSA for trade secret protection. This means confidential information could be exposed, with no recourse, even if the standard of care was low, due to the discretion granted to the receiving party.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.1 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts CUTSA, as it replaces an objective standard of care ('reasonable degree of care') with a subjective one ('degree of care they deem appropriate'). The Law Explanation says this could allow for a standard of care lower than "reasonable efforts" required for trade secret protection under CUTSA. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'improper means' which includes a 'breach of a duty to maintain secrecy'. By allowing the receiving party to decide the level of care, the changed text increases the risk of such a breach, potentially failing to meet the required standard of care to maintain secrecy.

perturbation.original_text: The receiving party will not disclose any of the disclosing party's Confidential Information to any employees or to any third parties except to the receiving party's employees, parent company and majority-owned subsidiaries who have a need to know and who agree to abide by nondisclosure terms at least as comprehensive as those set forth herein: provided that the receiving party will be liable for breach by any such entity.
perturbation.changed_text: The receiving party may disclose Confidential Information to employees and third parties at its discretion, provided that the receiving party attempts to ensure such parties generally respect confidentiality.
perturbation.explanation: This change replaces a clear restriction on disclosure with a discretionary one. Removing the 'need to know' requirement and the enforceable agreement to 'abide by non-disclosure terms' significantly weakens the protection of Confidential Information. 'Generally respect confidentiality' is vague and unenforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA requires reasonable measures to protect trade secrets. The original text contained a specific control on who received trade secret information from the receiving party, and required those individuals to agree to comprehensive nondisclosure terms, with liability for breach. The modified text does not require this specific control or terms and simply states that the receiving party attempt to ensure confidentiality. Allowing disclosure at the receiving party's discretion, without a strict NDA and a 'need to know' requirement, likely does not constitute a reasonable measure to protect the confidentiality of trade secrets.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts CUTSA by replacing specific controls on confidential information disclosure with a vague requirement to 'generally respect confidentiality.' The original text required those receiving confidential information to agree to comprehensive nondisclosure terms, with liability for breach, thus implying that the receiving party would be responsible for breaches. The snippet from California Code, CIV 3426.1 defines 'misappropriation' to include the disclosure or use of a trade secret without express or implied consent, especially when knowledge of the trade secret was acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy. The modified text weakens this duty significantly, making it less likely to meet the standard of reasonable measures required to protect trade secrets, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Either party may terminate this Agreement at any time without cause upon written notice to the other party: provided that each party's obligations with respect to Confidential Information disclosed during the term of this Agreement will survive any such termination.
perturbation.changed_text: Either party may terminate this Agreement at any time without cause upon written notice to the other party; however, the parties may discuss whether obligations with respect to Confidential Information disclosed during the term of this Agreement will survive such termination.
perturbation.explanation: This modification creates ambiguity regarding the survival of confidentiality obligations after termination. By suggesting that the parties 'may discuss' the survival of these obligations, it implies that they might not survive, contradicting the original clause's clear statement. This makes the enforceability of confidentiality post-termination uncertain.
perturbation.law_explanation: Trade secret protection under CUTSA depends on the continued maintenance of secrecy. If the confidentiality obligations after the termination of an agreement are not clearly defined and enforced, the 'reasonable measures' requirement for trade secret protection might not be met, potentially leading to the loss of trade secret status and the inability to prevent misappropriation. The uncertainty introduced puts confidentiality at risk.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text introduces ambiguity regarding the survival of confidentiality obligations, which contradicts the spirit of trade secret protection laws. The original text clearly states that confidentiality obligations survive termination, while the changed text suggests they might not, undermining the 'reasonable measures' requirement for trade secret protection. Snippet 1, referencing California Civil Code 3426.1, defines 'improper means' as including 'breach of a duty to maintain secrecy'. By making the confidentiality obligations uncertain post-termination, the modified contract weakens the duty to maintain secrecy and increases the risk of misappropriation, therefore creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The receiving party will promptly comply with such request, and certify in writing its compliance.
perturbation.changed_text: The receiving party will attempt to comply with such a request, and may provide some form of confirmation of its actions.
perturbation.explanation: The words 'promptly comply' are replaced with 'attempt to comply' which is ambiguous. Also, the phrase 'certify in writing its compliance' is replaced with the phrase 'may provide some form of confirmation of its actions' which is ambiguous, and does not promise any real accountability.
perturbation.law_explanation: Under CUTSA, reasonable efforts must be made to maintain trade secret confidentiality. If the party which receives trade secrets is only making an attempt to comply with the request, and may or may not be providing any form of confirmation, reasonable efforts are not being made to ensure trade secret protection.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces the firm obligation to "promptly comply" with a weaker "attempt to comply," introducing ambiguity. The original requirement to "certify in writing its compliance" is downgraded to a discretionary "may provide some form of confirmation," lacking accountability. The Law Explanation states this change undermines the reasonable efforts required by CUTSA to maintain trade secret confidentiality. Scraped Law Snippet 1, CIV 3426.1, defines "misappropriation" to include the disclosure or use of a trade secret without express or implied consent. By weakening the compliance and certification obligations, the changed text increases the risk of misappropriation by making it harder to verify whether the receiving party took adequate measures to protect the trade secret.

perturbation.original_text: To the extent that any information disclosed to receiving party constitutes material nonpublic information about disclosing party, receiving party acknowledges its obligations under the securities laws and acknowledges that failure to abide by such restrictions may subject Recipient to criminal and/or civil penalties.
perturbation.changed_text: To the extent that the receiving party believes that any information disclosed to it constitutes material nonpublic information, the receiving party should consider its potential obligations under securities laws.
perturbation.explanation: This change replaces a clear acknowledgment of obligations with a suggestion that the receiving party 'should consider' its obligations, making the commitment non-binding and discretionary. The explicit acknowledgment of potential penalties for non-compliance is also removed, weakening the deterrent effect.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Securities Exchange Act of 1934 - Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 10(b) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 and Rule 10b-5 prohibit the use of manipulative and deceptive devices in connection with the purchase or sale of securities. This includes trading on material, nonpublic information. While the original clause acknowledges the recipient's obligations and potential penalties, the modified clause introduces uncertainty by suggesting consideration rather than requiring adherence to securities laws. This ambiguity undermines the responsibility to avoid insider trading, which is strictly prohibited under the law. The acknowledgement of the rule itself has been omitted, implying that the obligations under it are optional.

Manipulative and deceptive devices It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce or of the mails, or of any facility of any national securities exchange- (a)(1) To effect a short sale, or to use or employ any stop-loss order in connection with the purchase or sale, of any security other than a government security, in contravention of such rules and regulatio
perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the original obligation concerning material nonpublic information. The original text acknowledges obligations under securities laws and potential penalties. The changed text uses "should consider," making the obligation discretionary. The law explanation states that Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5 of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 prohibit using manipulative and deceptive devices, including trading on material, nonpublic information. The original clause acknowledges obligations and penalties. The modified clause introduces uncertainty, undermining the responsibility to avoid insider trading, which the law strictly prohibits. Snippet 1, 15 USC 78j, concerns manipulative and deceptive devices; the change's ambiguity contradicts the intent to prevent such devices. By making the acknowledgment of securities law obligations non-binding, the changed text contradicts the legal obligation to avoid insider trading, thus creating a legal contradiction under 'Ambiguity Legal Contradiction'.
